chunks.text: Morphology is the study of word structure. A morpheme is the smallest meaningful unit of language. Free morphemes like "book" stand alone; bound morphemes like the plural suffix "-s" must attach to a stem. Derivational morphemes such as "un-" and "-ness" create new words (happy -> unhappiness), while inflectional morphemes like "-ed" and "-ing" mark grammar without changing word class. English has only eight inflectional morphemes. Panini's Ashtadhyayi, an ancient grammar of Sanskrit, described morphological rules with remarkable precision and influenced modern linguistics through scholars like Leonard Bloomfield, who wrote the book Language in 1933.

Etymology notes. The word 'shampoo' comes from Hindi champo, meaning to press or massage. The word 'bungalow' comes from Hindi bangla, a Bengal-style house. The word 'loot' comes from Hindi lut, meaning plunder. These are all loanwords borrowed into English during the colonial period.

# Body and People World Welcome to the Body and People World! Here we discover hidden word roots — the little shared pieces inside many words. ## Denta and the root DENT The word root DENT means 'tooth'. Meet Denta, who keeps every tooth shiny. Denta smiles a big toothy smile! 😁 Denta loves teeth — sharp, white, strong. The word family of DENT includes dentist (a doctor for teeth), dental (about teeth), trident (a spear with three teeth). The words dentist, dental, trident all share the hidden piece DENT, which is why they all carry the meaning 'tooth'. Two more words in the DENT family are denture (a set of new teeth), indent (a little tooth-shaped notch). Long ago, DENT and the Indian word दन्त (danta) were the same word — they are true cousins. In Hindi we say दाँत (daant), and in Bengali দাঁত (daant), for 'tooth'. ## Ped and the root PED The word root PED means 'foot'. Meet Ped, who is all about feet! Ped hops, stamps and pedals everywhere. 🦶 Wherever there are feet, Ped is there. The word family of PED includes pedal (a lever you push with your foot), pedestrian (a person going on foot), tripod (a stand with three feet). The words pedal, pedestrian, tripod all share the hidden piece PED, which is why they all carry the meaning 'foot'. Two more words in the PED family are pedicure (care for the feet), centipede (a bug with a hundred feet). Long ago, PED and the Indian word पद (pada) were the same word — they are true cousins. In Hindi we say पैर / पद (pair / pad), and in Bengali পা / পদ (paa / pad), for 'foot'. ## Manny and the root MANU The word root MANU means 'hand'. Meet Manny, whose hands can make anything. Manny builds, writes and waves — all by hand. ✋ Two clever hands, ready to work. The word family of MANU includes manual (done by hand), manicure (care for the hands), manufacture (to make (first, by hand)). The words manual, manicure, manufacture all share the hidden piece MANU, which is why they all carry the meaning 'hand'. Two more words in the MANU family are manuscript (something written by hand), manage (to handle something). In Hindi, the word for 'hand' is हाथ (haath), and in Bengali it is হাত (haat). (These are your home words for the idea, not cousins of MANU.)

# Nature and Sky World Welcome to the Nature and Sky World! Here we discover hidden word roots — the little shared pieces inside many words. ## Aqua and the root AQUA The word root AQUA means 'water'. Meet Aqua, who carries water everywhere. Splash! Aqua loves rivers, rain and the sea. 💧 Wherever there is water, Aqua giggles. The word family of AQUA includes aquarium (a glass home for fish and water), aquatic (living in water), aqueduct (a bridge that carries water). The words aquarium, aquatic, aqueduct all share the hidden piece AQUA, which is why they all carry the meaning 'water'. Two more words in the AQUA family are aquanaut (a diver who explores underwater), aquaplane (to slide on water). In Hindi, the word for 'water' is जल / पानी (jal / paani), and in Bengali it is জল / পানি (jol / paani). (These are your home words for the idea, not cousins of AQUA.) ## Sol and the root SOL The word root SOL means 'sun'. Meet Sol, bright and warm like the sun. Sol shines a golden smile each morning. ☀️ Where Sol goes, the world lights up. The word family of SOL includes solar (about the sun), solarium (a sunny glass room), parasol (a shade against the sun). The words solar, solarium, parasol all share the hidden piece SOL, which is why they all carry the meaning 'sun'. Two more words in the SOL family are solstice (the day the sun stands highest or lowest), solar panel (a plate that drinks sunlight). Long ago, SOL and the Indian word सूर्य (sūrya) were the same word — they are true cousins. In Hindi we say सूरज (sooraj), and in Bengali সূর্য (surjo), for 'sun'. ## Luna and the root LUNA The word root LUNA means 'moon'. Meet Luna, soft and silver like the moon. Luna comes out at night to play. 🌙 Luna glows while the world sleeps. The word family of LUNA includes lunar (about the moon), lunatic (once meant 'moon-struck'), lunamoth (a moth pale as the moon). The words lunar, lunatic, lunamoth all share the hidden piece LUNA, which is why they all carry the meaning 'moon'. Two more words in the LUNA family are lunar eclipse (when the moon goes dark), interlunar (the dark time between moons). In Hindi, the word for 'moon' is चाँद (chaand), and in Bengali it is চাঁদ (chaand). (These are your home words for the idea, not cousins of LUNA.) ## Terra and the root TERR The word root TERR means 'earth, land'. Meet Terra, who loves the good brown earth. Terra grows gardens and shapes the land. 🌱 Every hill and field belongs to Terra. The word family of TERR includes terrain (the shape of the land), territory (an area of land), terrarium (a little glass garden of earth). The words terrain, territory, terrarium all share the hidden piece TERR, which is why they all carry the meaning 'earth, land'. Two more words in the TERR family are terrace (a flat step of land), subterranean (under the ground).

(an area of land), terrarium (a little glass garden of earth). The words terrain, territory, terrarium all share the hidden piece TERR, which is why they all carry the meaning 'earth, land'. Two more words in the TERR family are terrace (a flat step of land), subterranean (under the ground). In Hindi, the word for 'earth, land' is धरती (dharti), and in Bengali it is মাটি (maati). (These are your home words for the idea, not cousins of TERR.) ## Flora and the root FLOR The word root FLOR means 'flower'. Meet Flora, who makes flowers bloom. Flora touches a bud and — POP — a flower! 🌸 Petals and blossoms follow Flora everywhere. The word family of FLOR includes floral (covered in flowers), florist (a person who sells flowers), flora (all the plants of a place). The words floral, florist, flora all share the hidden piece FLOR, which is why they all carry the meaning 'flower'. Two more words in the FLOR family are flourish (to bloom and grow well), floral crown (a ring of flowers). In Hindi, the word for 'flower' is फूल (phool), and in Bengali it is ফুল (phul). (These are your home words for the idea, not cousins of FLOR.) ## Zoey and the root ZOO The word root ZOO means 'animal'. Meet Zoey, friend of every animal. Zoey speaks to lions, frogs and birds. 🦁🐸🐦 All the creatures come when Zoey calls. The word family of ZOO includes zoo (a home for many animals), zoology (the study of animals), zookeeper (a person who cares for zoo animals). The words zoo, zoology, zookeeper all share the hidden piece ZOO, which is why they all carry the meaning 'animal'. Two more words in the ZOO family are zoologist (a scientist who studies animals), protozoa (the tiniest animals). In Hindi, the word for 'animal' is जानवर (jaanwar), and in Bengali it is প্রাণী (praani). (These are your home words for the idea, not cousins of ZOO.)

# Numbers World Welcome to the Numbers World! Here we discover hidden word roots — the little shared pieces inside many words. ## Tri and the root TRI The word root TRI means 'three'. Meet Tri, a tiny magical friend. Whenever Tri touches something, it turns into three! One balloon... POP! Now there are three. 🎈🎈🎈 The word family of TRI includes triangle (a shape with three corners), tricycle (a cycle with three wheels), trio (three friends together). The words triangle, tricycle, trio all share the hidden piece TRI, which is why they all carry the meaning 'three'. Two more words in the TRI family are triplets (three babies born at once), tripod (a stand with three legs). Long ago, TRI and the Indian word त्रि (tri) were the same word — they are true cousins. In Hindi we say तीन (teen), and in Bengali তিন (tin), for 'three'. ## Uni and the root UNI The word root UNI means 'one'. Meet Uni, who loves being the only one. Uni says: “I am one of a kind!” One horn, one wheel, one team — always ONE. The word family of UNI includes unicorn (a horse with one horn), unicycle (a cycle with one wheel), uniform (one same dress for everyone). The words unicorn, unicycle, uniform all share the hidden piece UNI, which is why they all carry the meaning 'one'. Two more words in the UNI family are unite (to join into one), unique (the only one like it). In Hindi, the word for 'one' is एक (ek), and in Bengali it is এক (ek). (These are your home words for the idea, not cousins of UNI.) ## Bi and the root BI The word root BI means 'two'. Meet Bi, who always comes in twos. Bi loves pairs — two wheels, two eyes, two wings! Whatever Bi touches gets a partner. The word family of BI includes bicycle (a cycle with two wheels), binoculars (two tubes to see far), biped (an animal with two feet). The words bicycle, binoculars, biped all share the hidden piece BI, which is why they all carry the meaning 'two'. Two more words in the BI family are bilingual (speaking two languages), biweekly (every two weeks). Long ago, BI and the Indian word द्वि (dvi) were the same word — they are true cousins. In Hindi we say दो (do), and in Bengali দুই (dui), for 'two'. ## Octo and the root OCTO The word root OCTO means 'eight'. Meet Octo, the friend with eight of everything. Octo waves eight arms hello! 👋 Eight legs, eight sides — always eight. The word family of OCTO includes octopus (a sea animal with eight arms), octagon (a shape with eight sides), october (once the eighth month). The words octopus, octagon, october all share the hidden piece OCTO, which is why they all carry the meaning 'eight'. Two more words in the OCTO family are octave (eight notes in music), octet (a group of eight). Long ago, OCTO and the Indian word अष्ट (aṣṭa) were the same word —

octopus, octagon, october all share the hidden piece OCTO, which is why they all carry the meaning 'eight'. Two more words in the OCTO family are octave (eight notes in music), octet (a group of eight). Long ago, OCTO and the Indian word अष्ट (aṣṭa) were the same word — they are true cousins. In Hindi we say आठ (aath), and in Bengali আট (aat), for 'eight'. ## Cent and the root CENT The word root CENT means 'hundred'. Meet Cent, who loves the big number hundred! Cent counts and counts — all the way to 100. 💯 A hundred legs, a hundred years — that is Cent. The word family of CENT includes century (a hundred years), centipede (a bug with many (a hundred!) feet), percent (out of a hundred). The words century, centipede, percent all share the hidden piece CENT, which is why they all carry the meaning 'hundred'. Two more words in the CENT family are centenary (a hundredth birthday), centimetre (a hundredth of a metre). Long ago, CENT and the Indian word शत (śata) were the same word — they are true cousins. In Hindi we say सौ (sau), and in Bengali একশো (eksho), for 'hundred'. ## Nova and the root NOV The word root NOV means 'new'. Meet Nova, who loves everything new. Nova claps for fresh starts and new days. ✨ With Nova, today is always brand-new. The word family of NOV includes novel (new and fresh (also a new story-book)), novice (someone new at a thing), renovate (to make new again). The words novel, novice, renovate all share the hidden piece NOV, which is why they all carry the meaning 'new'. Two more words in the NOV family are innovate (to bring in something new), novelty (a brand-new thing). Long ago, NOV and the Indian word नव (nava) were the same word — they are true cousins. In Hindi we say नया (nayaa), and in Bengali নতুন (notun), for 'new'.
